title: Probing Chaos and Criticality with Observational Entropy and Finite-Resolution Measurements
authors: J. Bharathi Kannan, Sreeram PG, M. S. Santhanam
abstract: Coarse-grained measurements offer a scalable alternative to full state tomography for characterizing complex quantum dynamics. We show that observational entropy (OE), an information-theoretic entropy defined directly from finite-resolution measurement outcomes, provides a unified and experimentally accessible framework for quantifying chaos and probing criticality. From probing the insulator-metal crossover in the Aubry-Andre model to tracking the gradual destruction of Kolmogorov-Arnold-Moser tori in the Kicked Rotor, derivatives of OE provide an accurate and unified diagnostic of probing these transitions. In both cases, the critical points extracted from dynamical evolution and eigenstate analyses converge to the exact theoretical values once the observational resolution exceeds a finite threshold. In the chaotic limit, OE exhibits a linear behavior within the Ehrenfest time regime, and its slope defines an observable Lyapunov exponent. Using a Pretty Good Measurement correction to the Husimi phase-space distribution, this entropy-production rate quantitatively reproduces the classical Lyapunov exponent in both the standard and singular kicked rotors. Our results establish OE as a compact information-theoretic bridge between classical instability, quantum criticality, and realistic finite-resolution measurements.
summary: The paper introduces 'Observational Entropy' as a scalable tool to detect quantum phase transitions and quantify chaos. By using coarse-grained measurements, the authors show that one can identify localization and the destruction of KAM tori while accurately recovering classical Lyapunov exponents. This framework is particularly useful for characterizing complex quantum systems where full state tomography is impossible.
main_problem: How to accurately characterize quantum chaos, ergodicity breaking, and localization transitions using scalable, finite-resolution measurements instead of full state tomography.
main_result: Observational entropy (OE) provides a unified diagnostic where its derivatives identify transition types (KAM destruction vs. localization) and its growth rate reproduces classical Lyapunov exponents.
method: Coarse-graining probability distributions into macrostates and using a Pretty Good Measurement (PGM) correction to the Husimi phase-space distribution.
model_or_system: The study utilizes the Aubry-Andre model for localization transitions and the Standard and Singular Kicked Rotors for studying the transition from integrable to chaotic dynamics.
key_observables: Observational Entropy (OE), entropy derivatives (susceptibility and curvature), and the observable Lyapunov exponent.
important_parameters: Coarse-graining resolution (chi), kick strength (K), non-analyticity exponent (alpha), Ehrenfest time, and measurement shot noise.
main_assumptions: The accuracy of the method is limited by an intrinsic resolution bound and the statistical uncertainty arising from finite measurement shots.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the coarse-graining protocol and OE derivatives; Fig 2 demonstrates the accuracy of critical point estimates and finite-size scaling; Fig 3 compares momentum-space vs. phase-space OE growth and Lyapunov exponent extraction; Fig 4 illustrates the trade-off between resolution and shot noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Observational Entropy framework, applies it to the Aubry-Andre and Kicked Rotor models to identify phase transitions, demonstrates its ability to quantify classical chaos via phase-space measurements, and analyzes the impact of finite resolution and shot noise.
why_interesting: It provides a practical, information-theoretic bridge between classical chaos and quantum dynamics, offering an experimentally feasible way to probe complex dynamics in many-body systems and quantum simulators without full state reconstruction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Top: Schematic of measuring observational en- tropy for a time-evolved state with coarse-graining length χ. Bottom: (a) quasi-momentum averaged observational entropy ⟨Sχ⟩of the QKR versus kick strength K. (b) Entropy acceler- ation Aχ(K), which peaks near the classical KAM threshold. (c) ⟨Sχ⟩of the Aubry-Andr´e model versus λ. (d) Entropy susceptibility ξχ(λ), which dips near the self-dual localization transition. In all panels, results from long-time dynamics and eigenstate averages are in close agreement.

FIG. 2. Critical-point estimates obtained from derivatives of the observational entropy as a function of the coarse-graining length χ. (a) Kest c for the quantum kicked rotor from the peak of the entropy curvature ∂2 K⟨Sχ⟩(b) Estimated λc for the Aubry-Andr´e model from the peak of the entropy sus- ceptibility −∂λ⟨Sχ⟩, comparing dynamical and eigenstate av- erages. Dotted lines denote the exact critical points. (c,d) Finite-size scaling of the corresponding estimates for differ- ent Hilbert-space sizes N. For experimentally relevant sizes (N = 128), OE already yields accurate critical points, which converge rapidly to the exact values as N increases.

FIG. 3. Observational entropy growth and extracted Lya- punov exponents for the Quantum Kicked Rotor (QKR, left column) and Singular Kicked Rotor (SKR, right column). Top row: Time evolution of momentum-space (blue circles) and PGM phase-space (red squares) observational entropy SOE, with dashed line indicating the ehrenfest times corre- spondingly. Middle row: Lyapunov exponents λ as a func- tion of kick strength K and singularity parameter b. Black dashed lines show the classical maximal Lyapunov exponent λcl. Figure 4 quantifies the effect of finite measurement statistics on the extraction of the observable Lya- punov exponent from the PGM-based observational entropy. For each phase-space resolution Ncells, the exact PGM probabilities are sampled from a multi- nomial distribution using Nshots repetitions, and the resulting entropy is fitted within the shaded Ehrenfest window to obtain λ(PGM) OE . FIG. 4. Effect of finite measurement statistics on the PGM- based observable Lyapunov exponent for the quantum kicked rotor at K = 10. Top and middle panels: time evolution of the observational entropy for Ncells = 26 and Ncells = 29, re- spectively. Shaded regions indicate the Ehrenfest-time fitting window used to extract λ(PGM) OE . Bottom panel: extracted λ(PGM) OE as a function of the number of phase-space cells for different numbers of measurement shots. Black circles denote the exact result and the dash-dotted line marks the classical Lyapunov exponent, λcl = 1.619.
